title: Midterms Analysis: Abolitionist Candidates and Lessons Learned from Failed Initiatives
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman recaps the 2022 midterms for the abolitionist movement, naming newly elected abolitionist legislators in Iowa, Idaho, Alabama, and Indiana, then dissects why pro-life referendums in Kansas, Kentucky, and Montana failed. He argues weak, defensive messaging is political malpractice and urges abolitionists to put forward bold legislation and aggressive offense against pro-abortion politicians.
audiences: State legislator considering an abolition bill

questions.question: Which abolitionist candidates won state legislative races in the 2022 midterms?
questions.answer: Silberman names four explicit abolitionist victories: Zach Dieken in Iowa HD5, Scott Herndon in Idaho SD1, Ernie Yarbrough in Alabama's seventh district, and Lorissa Sweet in Indiana's 50th district. He also notes Dan Foreman appears to have unseated a Democrat in Idaho LD6, and that with Heather Scott's reelection Idaho will have abolition bills pending in both chambers.
questions.quote: For the Abolitionist Movement, there will be a few new abolitionist faces in state legislatures around the country in 2023.

questions.question: How decisively did Lorissa Sweet beat the Republican establishment in Indiana?
questions.answer: Sweet defeated incumbent Dan Leonard, a close ally of Speaker Todd Huston, in the primary with more than 56 percent of the vote despite being outraised roughly 20-to-one, then won the general by more than 54 points and has committed to carry the bill to abolish abortion in Indiana.
questions.quote: Leonard had close ties to Speaker Todd Huston and according to the Associated Press, outraised Sweet by an almost 20-to-one ratio. Still, Sweet defeated Leonard with more than 56 percent of the vote.

questions.question: What happened to abortion-related referendums on the 2022 ballot?
questions.answer: Three blue states (Michigan 57-43, California 65-35, Vermont 77-23) enshrined abortion as a constitutional right, while pro-life referendums failed in Kentucky 52-48 and Montana 53-47. Silberman frames this as continuing the trend of the Kansas Value Them Both defeat, arguing Dobbs energized pro-abortion turnout far more than pro-life turnout.
questions.quote: Abortion was protected in three blue states on Tuesday, as Michigan (57-to-43), California (65-to-35), and Vermont (77-to-23) voters chose to make abortion a Constitutional right.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call the Montana born-alive referendum a damning case study?
questions.answer: He uses Montana to argue that even the softest possible pro-life ask, simply requiring care for infants who survive abortion, cannot win on the ballot, exposing the bankruptcy of moderate compromise messaging. The fact that it lost in a deep-red state is, for him, decisive evidence against the weak-bills-and-soft-messaging strategy.
questions.quote: This is the weakest, softest, most milquetoast, pro-life compromise known to the abortion debate and it failed in a ruby red state.

questions.question: What does Silberman say Republicans who avoided the abortion issue accomplished?
questions.answer: He claims silence on abortion is both sin and political malpractice because it cedes the messaging ground to Democrats, and he observes that politicians who actually leaned into abortion messaging, even weak pro-life messaging, outperformed those who ran from the issue.
questions.quote: Running from the abortion issue is, of course, a sin before God, but it is also political malpractice.

questions.question: How did pro-abortion campaigners reframe modest pro-life referendums to defeat them?
questions.answer: Silberman observes that Democrats deliberately turn every abortion referendum into a referendum on total abolition, citing Kentucky pro-abortion campaigners who falsely told voters that Amendment 2 meant no abortions with no exceptions, even though the amendment would not have changed any existing law.
questions.quote: Democrats are going to make every abortion referendum a referendum on total abolition. In Kentucky, pro-abortion campaigners lied to voters, saying “Amendment 2 means no abortions, no exceptions.”

questions.question: What offensive posture does Silberman recommend abolitionists adopt against pro-abortion voters?
questions.answer: Rather than defending modest proposals from attacks calling them cruel total bans, he urges abolitionists to put forward bold legislation, brand pro-abortion voters and legislators as accomplices to murder, and show images of abortion to make voters work to suppress the truth, accepting mockery as the cost of obedience.
questions.quote: Instead of framing a pro-abortion vote as a failure to love them both, call pro-abortion voters and legislators accomplices to murder.

questions.question: What did Silberman flag as a tragic outcome in the one state where the red wave actually arrived?
questions.audience: State legislator considering an abolition bill
questions.answer: Florida Republicans gained four congressional seats and supermajorities in both legislative chambers, but party leaders reportedly intend to use that mandate to pass a heartbeat bill in 2023 rather than abolish abortion, meaning abortion will continue under the new GOP supermajority.
questions.quote: Tragically, rumors out of Florida indicate that party leaders intend to use their new supermajorities to move ahead with a heartbeat bill in 2023 rather than abolish abortion.

questions.question: What scriptural authority does Silberman invoke to justify aggressive exposure of the abortion industry?
questions.answer: He closes with Ephesians 5:11 to ground his call for offensive, image-bearing tactics, framing exposure of pro-abortion wickedness as a biblical command rather than an optional rhetorical strategy.
questions.quote: “Take no part in the unfruitful works of darkness, but instead expose them.” -Ephesians 5:11

related.title: Using the A-Word, Indiana Abolitionist John Jacob Unseats Incumbent Pro-Life Rep.
related.reason: Earlier Indiana primary win in the same playbook Lorissa Sweet replicated in 2022, showing the bipartisan-establishment-defeating pattern over multiple cycles.

related.title: John Jacob Wins!
related.reason: 2020 general election companion piece to the 2022 Indiana 50th district result discussed here.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho Rep. Brent Crane Insults and Intimidates Family, Promises to Keep Abortion Legal
related.reason: Background on the Idaho establishment Scott Herndon was running against when he won SD1.

related.title: Utah/Idaho Baptist Convention Calls UT-ID Governors, Legislature to Abolish Abortion
related.reason: Context for the Idaho ecclesial pressure that helped pave the way for Herndon and Heather Scott's abolition bills.

related.title: BIG: Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against GOP Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
related.reason: Companion 2022 election cycle coverage of abolitionist primary challenges against pro-life GOP incumbents.

related.title: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
related.reason: Later strategic response to the same referendum-failure problem Silberman diagnoses for Kansas, Kentucky, and Montana.

related.title: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
related.reason: Doctrinal extension of Silberman's argument that silence on abortion or support for pro-abortion politicians is sin.
